title: Exploring Sulfate as an Alternative Electron Acceptor: A Potential Strategy to Mitigate N<sub>2</sub>O Emissions in Upland Arable Soils.
author_string: Lee, Hyun Ho, Kim, Hanbeen, Park, Ye Lim, Horn, Marcus A, Kim, Jeongeun et al.
abstract: Agricultural activities are a significant source of nitrous oxide (N<sub>2</sub>O), accounting for approximately 60% of global emissions, highlighting the urgent need for innovative strategies to mitigate N<sub>2</sub>O emissions. Microbes conserve nearly as much energy with nitrate (NO<sub>3</sub> <sup>-</sup>) as oxygen (O<sub>2</sub>) respiration under limited O<sub>2</sub> availability. Thus, microorganisms prioritize NO<sub>3</sub> <sup>-</sup>, limiting exploration of alternative electron acceptors (EAs) to inhibit N<sub>2</sub>O emissions through NO<sub>3</sub> <sup>-</sup> respiration in upland arable soils. Current approaches remain insufficient, and the interactions between alternative EA reduction and pathways for N<sub>2</sub>O emissions remain poorly understood. This study evaluated oxidized iron, manganese, and sulfate as alternative EAs to reduce N<sub>2</sub>O emissions, along with the effects of zero-valent metals (ZVMs). Metal sulfates (MSs) significantly minimized N<sub>2</sub>O emissions by inhibiting denitrification rather than altering nitrification in microcosms, as supported by isotope mapping and inorganic nitrogen concentrations. Among others, putative complete denitrifiers, N<sub>2</sub>O reducers, and sulfate reducers were stimulated, whereas ZVMs stimulated N<sub>2</sub>O emissions and 16S rRNA gene abundance. Moreover, the abundance of denitrifier-related genes (nirK, nirS, norB, and nosZ) consistently decreased under MS treatments, while dsrA mRNA abundance significantly increased. Sulfate (SO<sub>4</sub> <sup>2-</sup>) addition reshaped the soil microbial community by enriching sulfur-cycling taxa-including sulfate-reducing and sulfur-oxidizing bacteria-while suppressing nitrifiers such as Nitrospira, potentially disrupting nitrification-denitrification coupling. Ureibacillus thermosphaerius, harboring genes for denitrification and SO<sub>4</sub> <sup>2-</sup> reduction, increased under MS treatment. These shifts likely redirected electron flow toward SO<sub>4</sub> <sup>2-</sup> respiration, reducing NO<sub>3</sub> <sup>-</sup> utilization and contributing to N<sub>2</sub>O mitigation. Field-based manipulation experiments over 2 years demonstrated the feasibility of MSs in upland arable soils, reducing yield-scaled N<sub>2</sub>O emissions by 21.5% without compromising crop yields. A systematic literature review and meta-analysis revealed that SO<sub>4</sub> <sup>2-</sup> application mitigated N<sub>2</sub>O emissions by an average of 9%, with over 70% of observations showing a decreasing trend, underscoring its potential as an effective soil amendment for sustainable agriculture.
